dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 for chest pain at the city jail.
Hi, my name is Sam, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She's been having chest pain. Jail staff brought her in because of very high blood pressure.
Ma'am, can you tell me what the date is today?
My name's Mary. It's the 15th.
Can you show me your big toe on your left foot?
Yep, right here.
Ma'am, does anything feel blocked or uncomfortable in your throat?
Respirations are regular, rate 18 and depth normal. No cyanosis.
Pulse is 90, regular. Peripheral pulses are present. Skin is warm, dry and pink.
You're alert with full eye opening. No signs of distress.
You're alert, verbal response clear, and you responded to motor commands. Good A&O.
You mentioned chest pain. Can you tell us where exactly you're feeling it and what it feels like?
We noted on your chart a history of atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, and high blood pressure.
It's been on and off for a few days. It flared up worse today.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad was the pain before you got nitroglycerin?
Ma'am, did anything help or make the pain worse before we arrived?
It was a 10. The nitro made it go completely away.
Initial blood pressure was 214 over 104. Current is 191 over 101.
Did the pain radiate anywhere else like your arm, back, or jaw?
No, it was all in the center of my chest.
You were given 324 mg of aspirin and 0.4 mg nitroglycerin sublingual earlier by medical staff.
You mentioned you wouldn't normally go to the hospital for this. What prompted today's 911 call?
It spiked to 220 over 110, so they insisted on calling EMS.
Your pulse remains stable at 88.
Breathing remains 18 and regular.
Oxygen saturation on room air is currently 98%.
We are placing you on four-lead ECG monitoring as part of standard protocol for chest pain.
You take medications for your heart conditions, including lisinopril, metoprolol, clonidine, and apixaban.
Your medical history includes congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and primary hypertension.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event - A&Ox4. Good Glasgow Coma Scale (15).
A 12-lead ECG showed signs of left ventricular hypertrophy, STT changes in leads I/aVL/V5/V6, and T wave abnormalities in II/aVF consistent with possible lateral and inferior ischemia.
We're starting an IV line in your arm with an 18-gauge catheter and a saline lock.
Blood pressure is 196 over 123. Pulse is 88 and oxygen saturation at 99%.
We will follow protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain due to your medical history, vitals, and ECG findings.
You will be transported cold and in semi-Fowler's position to VCU for further evaluation.
We're now moving you to the stretcher and securing all five restraint straps.
We've completed treatments, paperwork, and prepared for handoff to the VCU ED.
Your transport was arranged against your wishes under the custody of the Richmond Sheriff's Department.
No significant changes during transport. Pulse 84, blood pressure 186/92, and oxygen saturation 97%.
